Amara goes to bed at 11pm and sleeps a full 7.5 hours, ending well before 6am the next calendar day; she wakes naturally with full energy for the next day.

diary: Monday started crisp and clear—perfect weather for a drive into the city. I was cataloging some mid-century periodicals when the World Cup buzz hit, and honestly, I couldn't resist slipping out to Matt's for the match. The bar was already packed with USA fans by 11am, and I grabbed a spot near the window with a sandwich. The energy was infectious, even if the game wasn't exactly thrilling. Afterward, I wandered through Pike Place, lost an hour browsing vintage typewriters and old cookbooks—the kind of aimless wandering I don't get to do often. Back at the library, the afternoon felt a bit heavy after all that stimulation. By evening, I was relieved to be home, checking on my brewing batch and listening to a podcast about archival digitization. The day had a strange rhythm to it—pulled between duty and impulse.
thought_process: The light rail closure didn't affect me since I drive, but it made me aware of how the city was shifting around maintenance work. The World Cup match tempted me partly because of FOMO—everyone was talking about it—but also because I genuinely wanted to feel part of something collective. I rationalized the long lunch break as justified; archival work is solitary and draining. The market browse was pure indulgence, almost guilt-tinged, but necessary. By afternoon, I felt the pendulum swing back toward routine and responsibility. My mood dipped slightly during those final work hours—that post-adventure letdown. Evening rituals with the brewing and podcasts grounded me again, reminding me that my real pleasures are often small and domestic.
world_event_prompt: Sunny Monday in early June, high of 74F. FIFA World Cup group stage match (USA vs Wales) airs at 11am PDT — bars downtown open early. Light Rail closed between Westlake and University District 9am-3pm for maintenance.
